(**MLB**) is a professional [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") league in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") composed of 30 teams, divided equally between the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") (NL) and the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") (AL), with 29 in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and 1 in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). MLB is one of the [major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada") and is considered the premier professional baseball league in the world.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Each team plays 162 games per season, with [Opening Day](/wiki/Opening_Day "Opening Day") held during the last week of March or the first week of April. Six teams in each league then advance to a four-round [postseason tournament](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_postseason "Major League Baseball postseason") in October, culminating in the [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series"), a [best-of-seven](/wiki/Best-of-seven "Best-of-seven") championship series between the two league champions first played in 1903. MLB is headquartered in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[7]](#cite_note-AboutMLB-7)

**Major League Baseball** (**MLB**) is a professional [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") league in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") composed of 30 teams, divided equally between the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") (NL) and the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") (AL), with 29 in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and 1 in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). MLB is one of the [major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada") and is considered the premier professional baseball league in the world.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Each team plays 162 games per season, with [Opening Day](/wiki/Opening_Day "Opening Day") held during the last week of March or the first week of April. Six teams in each league then advance to a four-round [postseason tournament](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_postseason "Major League Baseball postseason") in October, culminating in the [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series"), a [best-of-seven](/wiki/Best-of-seven "Best-of-seven") championship series between the two league champions first played in 1903. MLB is headquartered in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[7]](#cite_note-AboutMLB-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/1869HarpersWeeklyBaseballRedStockings.jpg/250px-1869HarpersWeeklyBaseballRedStockings.jpg)](/wiki/File:1869HarpersWeeklyBaseballRedStockings.jpg)

The Cincinnati Red Stockings were the first professional major league team.

Formed in 1876 and 1901, respectively, the NL and AL cemented their cooperation with the National Agreement in 1903, making MLB the oldest major professional sports league in the United States and Canada.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) They remained legally separate entities until 2000, when they merged into a single organization led by the [commissioner of baseball](/wiki/Commissioner_of_baseball "Commissioner of baseball").[[3]](#cite_note-MLBCommissioner-3)[[7]](#cite_note-AboutMLB-7)[[16]](#cite_note-2000nl-16)[[4]](#cite_note-Chass-4) Baseball's first all-professional team, the [Cincinnati Red Stockings](/wiki/Cincinnati_Red_Stockings "Cincinnati Red Stockings"), was founded in 1869.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) The first few decades of professional baseball saw rivalries between leagues, and players often jumped from one team or league to another.[[19]](#cite_note-19) These practices were essentially ended by the National Agreement of 1903, in which AL and NL agreed to respect each other's player contracts, including the contentious [reserve clause](/wiki/Reserve_clause "Reserve clause"), which bound players to their teams.

The period from about 1900 to 1920 was the [dead-ball era](/wiki/Dead-ball_era "Dead-ball era"), when [home runs](/wiki/Home_run "Home run") were rarely hit.[[20]](#cite_note-:4-20) Professional baseball was rocked by the [Black Sox Scandal](/wiki/Black_Sox_Scandal "Black Sox Scandal"), a conspiracy to [fix](/wiki/Match_fixing "Match fixing") the [1919 World Series](/wiki/1919_World_Series "1919 World Series"). Baseball survived the scandal, albeit with major changes in its governance as the relatively weak National Commission was replaced with a powerful commissioner of baseball with near-unlimited authority over the sport. MLB rose in popularity in the decade following the Black Sox Scandal, and unlike major leagues in other sports, it endured the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_the_United_States "Great Depression in the United States") and [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") without any of its teams folding. Shortly after the war, [Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson") broke [baseball's color barrier](/wiki/Baseball_color_line "Baseball color line").

Some teams [moved to different cities](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_relocations_of_1950s%E2%80%931960s "Major League Baseball relocations of 1950s–1960s") in the 1950s and 1960s. The AL and NL added eight clubs in the 1960s, which started the [expansion](/wiki/Expansion_team "Expansion team") era: two in 1961, two in 1962, and four in 1969.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Player discontent with established labor practices, especially the reserve clause, led to the organization of the [Major League Baseball Players Association](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_Players_Association "Major League Baseball Players Association") to [collectively bargain](/wiki/Collectively_bargain "Collectively bargain") with the owners, which in turn led to the introduction of [free agency](/wiki/Free_agency "Free agency") in baseball. Modern stadiums with [artificial turf](/wiki/Artificial_turf "Artificial turf") surfaces began to change the game in the 1970s and 1980s. Home runs began to dominate the game during the 1990s. In the mid-2000s, media reports disclosed the use of [anabolic steroids](/wiki/Anabolic_steroid "Anabolic steroid") among MLB players; a 2006–07 investigation produced the [Mitchell Report](/wiki/Mitchell_Report "Mitchell Report"), which found that many players had used steroids and other [performance-enhancing substances](/wiki/Performance-enhancing_substance "Performance-enhancing substance"), including at least one player from each team.

MLB is the third-wealthiest [professional sports](/wiki/Professional_sports "Professional sports") league in the world [by revenue](/wiki/List_of_professional_sports_leagues_by_revenue "List of professional sports leagues by revenue") after the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA).[[23]](#cite_note-urlReport:_MLB_revenue_for_2019_season_a_record_$10.7_billion-23)[[24]](#cite_note-url11_Most_Profitable_Sports_Leagues_-_Their_Value_Will_Surprise_You_-_Athletic_Panda_Sports_Editors-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) Baseball games are broadcast on television, radio, and the internet throughout North America and in several other countries. MLB has the [highest total season attendance](/wiki/List_of_attendance_figures_at_domestic_professional_sports_leagues "List of attendance figures at domestic professional sports leagues") of any sports league in the world; in 2025, it drew 71.4 million spectators.[[26]](#cite_note-26) MLB also oversees [Minor League Baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball"), which comprises lower-tier teams affiliated with the major league clubs, the [MLB Draft League](/wiki/MLB_Draft_League "MLB Draft League"), a hybrid amateur-professional showcase league, and the [Arizona Fall League](/wiki/Arizona_Fall_League "Arizona Fall League"), an off-season showcase league for minor league players. MLB and the [World Baseball Softball Confederation](/wiki/World_Baseball_Softball_Confederation "World Baseball Softball Confederation") jointly manage the international [World Baseball Classic](/wiki/World_Baseball_Classic "World Baseball Classic") tournament. The [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") have the [most championships](/wiki/List_of_World_Series_champions "List of World Series champions") with 27. The reigning champions are the [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers"), who defeated the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays") in the [2025 World Series](/wiki/2025_World_Series "2025 World Series").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of baseball in the United States](/wiki/History_of_baseball_in_the_United_States "History of baseball in the United States")

### Founding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding")]

In the 1860s, aided by soldiers playing the game in camp during the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), "New York"-style baseball expanded into a national game and spawned baseball's first governing body, the [National Association of Base Ball Players](/wiki/National_Association_of_Base_Ball_Players "National Association of Base Ball Players") (NABBP). The NABBP existed as an amateur league for 12 years. By 1867, more than 400 clubs were members. Most of the strongest clubs remained those based in the northeastern United States. For [professional baseball](/wiki/Professional_baseball "Professional baseball")'s founding year, MLB uses the year 1869—when the first professional team, the [Cincinnati Red Stockings](/wiki/Cincinnati_Red_Stockings "Cincinnati Red Stockings"), was established.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

A schism developed between professional and amateur ballplayers after the founding of the Cincinnati club. The NABBP split into an amateur organization and a professional organization. The [National Association of Professional Base Ball Players](/wiki/National_Association_of_Professional_Base_Ball_Players "National Association of Professional Base Ball Players"), often known as the National Association (NA), was formed in 1871.[[28]](#cite_note-base_ball-28) Its amateur counterpart disappeared after only a few years.[[29]](#cite_note-Rader-29) The modern [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") and [Atlanta Braves](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves") franchises trace their histories back to the National Association of Professional Base Ball Players in the 1870s.[[30]](#cite_note-Spatz-30)

In 1876, the National League of Professional Base Ball Clubs (later known as the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") or NL) was established after the NA proved ineffective. The league placed its emphasis on clubs rather than on players. Clubs could now enforce player contracts, preventing players from jumping to higher-paying clubs. Clubs were required to play the full schedule of games instead of forfeiting scheduled games when the club was no longer in the running for the league championship, which happened frequently under the NA. A concerted effort was made to curb gambling on games, which was leaving the validity of results in doubt. The first game in the NL—on Saturday, April 22, 1876 (at [Jefferson Street Grounds](/wiki/Jefferson_Street_Grounds "Jefferson Street Grounds") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"))—is often pointed to as the beginning of MLB.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/1896_Baltimore_Orioles.jpg/250px-1896_Baltimore_Orioles.jpg)](/wiki/File:1896_Baltimore_Orioles.jpg)

National League [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles_(19th_century) "Baltimore Orioles (19th century)"), 1896

The early years of the NL were tumultuous, with threats from rival leagues and a rebellion by players against the hated "reserve clause", which restricted the free movement of players between clubs. Teams came and went; 1882 was the first season where the league's membership was the same as the preceding season's, and only four franchises survived to see 1900. Competitor leagues formed regularly and also disbanded regularly. The most successful was the [American Association](/wiki/American_Association_(19th_century) "American Association (19th century)") (1882–1891), sometimes called the "beer and whiskey league" for its tolerance of the sale of alcoholic beverages to spectators. For several years, the NL and American Association champions met in a postseason championship series—the first attempt at a [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series"). The two leagues merged in 1892 as a single 12-team NL, but the NL dropped four teams after the 1899 season. This led to the formation of the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") (AL) in 1901 under president [Ban Johnson](/wiki/Ban_Johnson "Ban Johnson"), and the resulting bidding war for players led to widespread contract-breaking and legal disputes.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The war between the AL and NL caused shock waves throughout the baseball world. At a meeting at the Leland Hotel in Chicago in 1901, the other baseball leagues negotiated a plan to maintain their independence. A new National Association was formed to oversee these minor leagues.[[33]](#cite_note-hotel-33)

After 1902, the NL, AL, and NA signed a new National Agreement which tied independent contracts to the reserve-clause contracts. The agreement also set up a formal classification system for minor leagues, the forerunner of today's system that was refined by [Branch Rickey](/wiki/Branch_Rickey "Branch Rickey").[[34]](#cite_note-nabpl-34)

#### Other recognized leagues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Other recognized leagues")]

Several other early defunct baseball leagues are considered major leagues, and their statistics and records are included with those of the two modern major leagues. In 1969, the Special Baseball Records Committee of Major League Baseball officially recognized six major leagues: the National League, American League, American Association, [Union Association](/wiki/Union_Association "Union Association") (1884), [Players' League](/wiki/Players%27_League "Players' League") (1890), and [Federal League](/wiki/Federal_League "Federal League") (1914–1915).[[35]](#cite_note-35) The status of the National Association as a major league has been [a point of dispute](/wiki/National_Association_as_a_major_league "National Association as a major league") among baseball researchers; while its statistics are not recognized by Major League Baseball, its statistics *are* included with those of other major leagues by some baseball reference websites, such as [Retrosheet](/wiki/Retrosheet "Retrosheet").[[36]](#cite_note-36) Some researchers, including [Nate Silver](/wiki/Nate_Silver "Nate Silver"), dispute the major-league status of the Union Association by pointing out that franchises came and went and that the St. Louis club was deliberately "stacked"; the St. Louis club was owned by the league's president and it was the only club that was close to major-league caliber.[[37]](#cite_note-union-37)

In December 2020, Major League Baseball announced its recognition of seven leagues within [Negro league baseball](/wiki/Negro_league_baseball "Negro league baseball") as major leagues: the [first](/wiki/Negro_National_League_(1920%E2%80%931931) "Negro National League (1920–1931)") and [second Negro National Leagues](/wiki/Negro_National_League_(1933%E2%80%931948) "Negro National League (1933–1948)") (1920–1931 and 1933–1948), the [Eastern Colored League](/wiki/Eastern_Colored_League "Eastern Colored League") (1923–1928), the [American Negro League](/wiki/American_Negro_League "American Negro League") (1929), the [East–West League](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_League "East–West League") (1932), the [Negro Southern League](/wiki/Negro_Southern_League_(1920%E2%80%931936) "Negro Southern League (1920–1936)") (1932), and the [Negro American League](/wiki/Negro_American_League "Negro American League") (1937–1948).[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) In 2021, baseball reference website [Baseball-Reference.com](/wiki/Baseball-Reference.com "Baseball-Reference.com") began to include statistics from those seven leagues into their major-league statistics.[[40]](#cite_note-40) In May 2024, Major League Baseball announced that it was "absorbing the available Negro Leagues numbers into the official historical record."[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### Dead-ball era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Dead-ball era")]

Main article: [Dead-ball era](/wiki/Dead-ball_era "Dead-ball era")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/T205_Cy_Young.jpg/250px-T205_Cy_Young.jpg)](/wiki/File:T205_Cy_Young.jpg)

Cy Young, 1911 baseball card

The period between 1900 and 1919 is commonly referred to as the "dead-ball era".[[20]](#cite_note-:4-20) Games of this era tended to be low-scoring and were often dominated by pitchers, such as [Walter Johnson](/wiki/Walter_Johnson "Walter Johnson"), [Cy Young](/wiki/Cy_Young "Cy Young"), [Christy Mathewson](/wiki/Christy_Mathewson "Christy Mathewson"), [Mordecai Brown](/wiki/Mordecai_Brown "Mordecai Brown"), and [Grover Cleveland Alexander](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland_Alexander "Grover Cleveland Alexander"). The term also accurately describes the condition of the baseball itself. The baseball used American rather than the modern Australian wool yarn and was not wound as tightly, affecting the distance that it would travel.[[42]](#cite_note-McNeil-42) More significantly, balls were kept in play until they were mangled, soft and sometimes lopsided. During this era, a baseball cost $3,[[43]](#cite_note-43) (equivalent to $56 in 2025), and owners were reluctant to purchase new balls. Fans were expected to throw back fouls and (rare) home runs. Baseballs also became stained with tobacco juice, grass, and mud, and sometimes the juice of licorice, which some players would chew for the purpose of discoloring the ball.[[44]](#cite_note-Keating-44)

Also, pitchers could manipulate the ball through the use of the [spitball](/wiki/Spitball "Spitball") (In 1921, use of this pitch was restricted to a few pitchers with a [grandfather clause](/wiki/Grandfather_clause "Grandfather clause")). Additionally, many ballparks had large dimensions, such as the [West Side Grounds](/wiki/West_Side_Grounds "West Side Grounds") of the Chicago Cubs, which was 560 feet (170 m) to the center field fence, and the [Huntington Avenue Grounds](/wiki/Huntington_Avenue_Grounds "Huntington Avenue Grounds") of the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox"), which was 635 feet (194 m) to the center field fence, thus home runs were rare, and "small ball" tactics such as [singles](/wiki/Single_(baseball) "Single (baseball)"), [bunts](/wiki/Bunt_(baseball) "Bunt (baseball)"), [stolen bases](/wiki/Stolen_base "Stolen base"), and the [hit-and-run](/wiki/Hit_and_run_(baseball) "Hit and run (baseball)") play dominated the strategies of the time.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Hitting methods like the [Baltimore chop](/wiki/Baltimore_chop "Baltimore chop") were used to increase the number of infield singles.[[46]](#cite_note-46) On a successful Baltimore chop, the batter hits the ball forcefully into the ground, causing it to bounce so high that the batter reaches first base before the ball can be fielded and thrown to the first baseman.[[47]](#cite_note-Lieb-47)

The adoption of the foul strike rule—in the NL in 1901, in the AL two years later—quickly sent baseball from a high-scoring game to one where scoring runs became a struggle. Before this rule, foul balls were not counted as strikes: a batter could foul off any number of pitches with no strikes counted against him; this gave an enormous advantage to the batter.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

After the [1919 World Series](/wiki/1919_World_Series "1919 World Series") between the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox") and Cincinnati Reds, baseball was rocked by allegations of a [game-fixing](/wiki/Game_fixing "Game fixing") scheme known as the [Black Sox Scandal](/wiki/Black_Sox_Scandal "Black Sox Scandal"). Eight players—["Shoeless" Joe Jackson](/wiki/Shoeless_Joe_Jackson "Shoeless Joe Jackson"), [Eddie Cicotte](/wiki/Eddie_Cicotte "Eddie Cicotte"), [Claude "Lefty" Williams](/wiki/Lefty_Williams "Lefty Williams"), [George "Buck" Weaver](/wiki/Buck_Weaver "Buck Weaver"), [Arnold "Chick" Gandil](/wiki/Arnold_%22Chick%22_Gandil "Arnold \"Chick\" Gandil"), [Fred McMullin](/wiki/Fred_McMullin "Fred McMullin"), [Charles "Swede" Risberg](/wiki/Swede_Risberg "Swede Risberg"), and [Oscar "Happy" Felsch](/wiki/Oscar_%22Happy%22_Felsch "Oscar \"Happy\" Felsch")—intentionally lost the World Series in exchange for a ring worth $100,000 (equivalent to $1,860,000 in 2025).[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) Despite being acquitted, all were permanently banned from MLB.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Rise in popularity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rise in popularity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Babe_Ruth_by_Paul_Thompson%2C_1920.jpg/250px-Babe_Ruth_by_Paul_Thompson%2C_1920.jpg)](/wiki/File:Babe_Ruth_by_Paul_Thompson,_1920.jpg)

[Babe Ruth](/wiki/Babe_Ruth "Babe Ruth"), often considered the greatest baseball player of all time, in 1920

Baseball's popularity increased in the 1920s and 1930s. The 1920 season was notable for the death of [Ray Chapman](/wiki/Ray_Chapman "Ray Chapman") of the [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/Cleveland_Indians "Cleveland Indians"). Chapman, who was struck in the head by a pitch and died a few hours later, became the only MLB player to die of an on-field injury.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Both leagues quickly began to require the use of new, white baseballs whenever a ball became scuffed or dirty, helping bring the "dead-ball" era to an end.

The following year, the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") made their first World Series appearance.[[53]](#cite_note-Plaque-53) By the end of the 1930s, the team had appeared in 11 World Series, winning eight of them.[[54]](#cite_note-BR-54) Yankees slugger [Babe Ruth](/wiki/Babe_Ruth "Babe Ruth") had set the single-season home run record in 1927, hitting 60 home runs; breaking his own record of 29 home runs.[[55]](#cite_note-Swat-55)

Impacted negatively by the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_the_United_States "Great Depression in the United States"), baseball's popularity had begun a downward turn in the early 1930s. By 1932, only two MLB teams turned a profit. Attendance had fallen, due at least in part to a 10% federal amusement tax added to baseball ticket prices. Baseball owners cut their [rosters](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_rosters "Major League Baseball rosters") from 25 men to 23, and even the best players took pay cuts. Team executives were innovative in their attempts to survive, creating night games, broadcasting games live by radio, and rolling out promotions such as free admission for women. Throughout the Great Depression, no MLB teams moved or folded.[[56]](#cite_note-Belson-56)

### World War II era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: World War II era")]

The onset of World War II created a shortage of professional baseball players, as more than 500 men left MLB teams to serve in the military. Many of them played on service baseball teams that entertained military personnel in the US or in the Pacific. MLB teams of this time largely consisted of young men, older players, and those with a military classification of [4F](/wiki/4F_(military_conscription) "4F (military conscription)"), indicating mental, physical, or moral unsuitability for service. Men like [Pete Gray](/wiki/Pete_Gray "Pete Gray"), a one-armed outfielder, got the chance to advance to the major leagues. However, MLB rosters did not include any black players through the end of the war.[[57]](#cite_note-War-57) Black players, many of whom served in the war, were still restricted to playing [Negro league baseball](/wiki/Negro_league_baseball "Negro league baseball").[[58]](#cite_note-Martin-58)

[Wartime blackout restrictions](/wiki/Blackout_(wartime) "Blackout (wartime)"), designed to keep outdoor lighting at low levels, caused another problem for baseball. These rules limited traveling and night games to the point that the 1942 season was nearly canceled.[[58]](#cite_note-Martin-58) On January 14, 1942, MLB commissioner [Kenesaw Mountain Landis](/wiki/Kenesaw_Mountain_Landis "Kenesaw Mountain Landis") wrote to U.S. president [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt"), pleading for the continuation of baseball during the war. Roosevelt responded, "I honestly feel that it would be best for the country to keep baseball going. There will be fewer people unemployed and everybody will work longer hours and harder than ever before. And that means that they ought to have a chance for recreation and for taking their minds off their work even more than before."[[59]](#cite_note-59)

With the approval of President Roosevelt, spring training began in 1942 with few repercussions. The war interrupted the careers of stars including [Stan Musial](/wiki/Stan_Musial "Stan Musial"), [Bob Feller](/wiki/Bob_Feller "Bob Feller"), [Ted Williams](/wiki/Ted_Williams "Ted Williams"), and [Joe DiMaggio](/wiki/Joe_DiMaggio "Joe DiMaggio"), but baseball clubs continued to field their teams.[[60]](#cite_note-Weintraub-60)

### Breaking the color barrier

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Breaking the color barrier")]

Main article: [Baseball color line](/wiki/Baseball_color_line "Baseball color line")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Jackie_Robinson_No5_comic_book_cover.jpg/250px-Jackie_Robinson_No5_comic_book_cover.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jackie_Robinson_No5_comic_book_cover.jpg)

*[Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson")* comic book, 1951

[Branch Rickey](/wiki/Branch_Rickey "Branch Rickey"), president and general manager of the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers"), began making efforts to introduce a black baseball player to the previously all-white professional baseball leagues in the mid-1940s. He selected [Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson") from a list of promising [Negro league](/wiki/Negro_league "Negro league") players. After obtaining a commitment from Robinson to "turn the other cheek" to any [racial antagonism](/wiki/Racial_antagonism "Racial antagonism") directed at him, Rickey agreed to sign him to a contract for $600 a month (equivalent to $9,906 in 2025). In what was later referred to as "The Noble Experiment", Robinson was the first black baseball player in the International League since the 1880s, joining the Dodgers' farm club, the [Montreal Royals](/wiki/Montreal_Royals "Montreal Royals"), for the 1946 season.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

The following year, the Dodgers called up Robinson to the major leagues. On April 15, 1947, Robinson made his major league debut at Ebbets Field before a crowd of 26,623 spectators, including more than 14,000 black patrons. Black baseball fans began flocking to see the Dodgers when they came to town, abandoning the Negro league teams that they had followed exclusively. Robinson's promotion met a generally positive, although mixed, reception among newspaper writers and white major league players. Manager [Leo Durocher](/wiki/Leo_Durocher "Leo Durocher") informed his team, "I don't care if he is yellow or black or has stripes like a fucking zebra. I'm his manager and I say he plays."[[62]](#cite_note-62)

After a strike threat by some players, NL president [Ford C. Frick](/wiki/Ford_C._Frick "Ford C. Frick") and Commissioner [Happy Chandler](/wiki/Happy_Chandler "Happy Chandler") let it be known that any striking players would be suspended. Robinson received significant encouragement from several major-league players, including Dodgers teammate [Pee Wee Reese](/wiki/Pee_Wee_Reese "Pee Wee Reese") who said, "You can hate a man for many reasons. Color is not one of them."[[63]](#cite_note-Newman-63) That year, Robinson won the inaugural [Major League Baseball Rookie of the Year Award](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_Rookie_of_the_Year_Award "Major League Baseball Rookie of the Year Award") (separate NL and AL Rookie of the Year honors were not awarded until 1949).[[64]](#cite_note-ROY-64)

Less than three months later, [Larry Doby](/wiki/Larry_Doby "Larry Doby") became the first African-American to break the color barrier in the American League with the Cleveland Indians.[[65]](#cite_note-Doby-65) The next year, a number of other black players entered the major leagues. [Satchel Paige](/wiki/Satchel_Paige "Satchel Paige") was signed by the Indians and the Dodgers added star catcher [Roy Campanella](/wiki/Roy_Campanella "Roy Campanella") and [Don Newcombe](/wiki/Don_Newcombe "Don Newcombe"), who was later the first winner of the [Cy Young Award](/wiki/Cy_Young_Award "Cy Young Award") for his outstanding pitching.[[66]](#cite_note-Finkelman-66)

### Women in baseball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Women in baseball")]

Main article: [Women in baseball](/wiki/Women_in_baseball "Women in baseball")

MLB banned the signing of women to contracts in 1952, but that ban was lifted in 1992.[[67]](#cite_note-67) There have been no female MLB players since then.

### Relocation and expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Relocation and expansion")]

Main article: [Major League Baseball relocations of 1950s–1960s](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_relocations_of_1950s%E2%80%931960s "Major League Baseball relocations of 1950s–1960s")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/LA_Coliseum_1959_World_Series.jpg/330px-LA_Coliseum_1959_World_Series.jpg)](/wiki/File:LA_Coliseum_1959_World_Series.jpg)

[1959 World Series](/wiki/1959_World_Series "1959 World Series") action at the [Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Memorial_Coliseum "Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum")

From 1903 to 1952, the major leagues consisted of two eight-team leagues[[a]](#cite_note-68) whose 16 teams were located in ten cities, all in the northeastern and mid-western United States: New York City had three teams and [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), Chicago, [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), and [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis") each had two teams. St. Louis was the southernmost and westernmost city with a major league team. The longest possible road trip, from Boston to St. Louis, took about 24 hours by railroad. After a half-century of stability, starting in the 1950s, teams began to move out of cities with multiple teams into cities that had not had them before. From 1953 to 1955, three teams moved to new cities: the [Boston Braves](/wiki/Boston_Braves "Boston Braves") became the [Milwaukee Braves](/wiki/Milwaukee_Braves "Milwaukee Braves"), the [St. Louis Browns](/wiki/St._Louis_Browns "St. Louis Browns") became the [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles "Baltimore Orioles"), and the [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/Philadelphia_Athletics "Philadelphia Athletics") became the [Kansas City Athletics](/wiki/Kansas_City_Athletics "Kansas City Athletics").

The [1958 season](/wiki/1958_Major_League_Baseball_season "1958 Major League Baseball season") began to turn Major League Baseball into a nationwide league. [Walter O'Malley](/wiki/Walter_O%27Malley "Walter O'Malley"), owner of the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers") and "perhaps the most influential owner of baseball's early expansion era,"[[68]](#cite_note-69) moved his team to Los Angeles, marking the first major league franchise on the West Coast.[[69]](#cite_note-FYOL-70) O'Malley also helped persuade the rival [New York Giants](/wiki/History_of_the_New_York_Giants_(NL) "History of the New York Giants (NL)") to move west to become the San Francisco Giants. Giants owner [Horace Stoneham](/wiki/Horace_Stoneham "Horace Stoneham") had been contemplating a move to [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") amid slumping attendance at the aging [Polo Grounds](/wiki/Polo_Grounds "Polo Grounds") ballpark[[70]](#cite_note-71) when O'Malley invited him to meet [San Francisco mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_San_Francisco "Mayor of San Francisco") [George Christopher](/wiki/George_Christopher_(mayor) "George Christopher (mayor)") in New York.[[71]](#cite_note-WiW-72) After Stoneham was persuaded to move to California, *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine put O'Malley on its cover.[[72]](#cite_note-73) MLB commissioner [Ford C. Frick](/wiki/Ford_C._Frick "Ford C. Frick") had opposed the meeting,[[73]](#cite_note-74) but the dual moves proved successful for both franchises and for MLB.[[69]](#cite_note-FYOL-70) Had the Dodgers moved out west alone, the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals")—1,600 mi (2,575 km) away[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76)—would have been the closest NL team. Instead, the joint move made West Coast road trips economical for visiting teams.[[71]](#cite_note-WiW-72) The Dodgers set a single-game MLB attendance record in their first home appearance with 78,672 fans.[[71]](#cite_note-WiW-72)

In 1961, the first [Washington Senators](/wiki/History_of_the_Washington_Senators "History of the Washington Senators") franchise moved to [Minneapolis–Saint Paul](/wiki/Minneapolis%E2%80%93Saint_Paul "Minneapolis–Saint Paul") to become the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/Minnesota_Twins "Minnesota Twins"). Two new teams were added to the American League at the same time: the [Los Angeles Angels](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Angels "Los Angeles Angels") (who soon moved from downtown L.A. to nearby [Anaheim](/wiki/Anaheim "Anaheim")) and a new [Washington Senators](/wiki/Washington_Senators_(1961%E2%80%931971) "Washington Senators (1961–1971)") franchise. The NL added the [Houston Astros](/wiki/Houston_Astros "Houston Astros") and the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets") in 1962. The Astros (known as the "Colt .45s" during their first three seasons) became the first southern major league franchise since the [Louisville Colonels](/wiki/Louisville_Colonels "Louisville Colonels") folded in 1899 and the first franchise to be located along the [Gulf Coast](/wiki/Gulf_Coast "Gulf Coast"). The Mets established a reputation for futility by going 40–120 during their first season of play in the nation's media capital—and by playing only a little better in subsequent campaigns—but in their eighth season (1969) the Mets became the first of the 1960s expansion teams to play in the postseason, culminating in a World Series title over the heavily favored Baltimore Orioles.

In 1966, the major leagues moved to the "Deep South" when the Milwaukee Braves moved to Atlanta. In 1968, the Kansas City Athletics moved west to become the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics "Oakland Athletics"). In 1969, the American and National Leagues both [added two expansion franchises](/wiki/1969_Major_League_Baseball_expansion "1969 Major League Baseball expansion"). The American League added the [Seattle Pilots](/wiki/Seattle_Pilots "Seattle Pilots") (who became the [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers") after one disastrous season in Seattle) and the [Kansas City Royals](/wiki/Kansas_City_Royals "Kansas City Royals"). The NL added the first Canadian franchise, the [Montreal Expos](/wiki/Montreal_Expos "Montreal Expos"), as well as the [San Diego Padres](/wiki/San_Diego_Padres "San Diego Padres").

In 1972, the second Washington Senators moved to the [Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex") to become the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)"). In 1977, baseball [expanded again](/wiki/1977_Major_League_Baseball_expansion "1977 Major League Baseball expansion"), adding a second Canadian team, the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays"), as well as the [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/Seattle_Mariners "Seattle Mariners"). Subsequently, no new teams were added until 1993, and no teams moved until 2005.

### Pitching dominance and rule changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Pitching dominance and rule changes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/MLB_runs.png/500px-MLB_runs.png)](/wiki/File:MLB_runs.png)

Graph showing, by year, the average number of runs per MLB game

By the late 1960s, the balance between pitching and hitting had swung in favor of the pitchers. In 1968—later nicknamed "the year of the pitcher"[[76]](#cite_note-yearofthepitcher-77)—Boston Red Sox player [Carl Yastrzemski](/wiki/Carl_Yastrzemski "Carl Yastrzemski") won the American League batting title with an average of just .301, the lowest in MLB history.[[77]](#cite_note-yaz-78) [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers") pitcher [Denny McLain](/wiki/Denny_McLain "Denny McLain") won 31 games, making him the only pitcher to win 30 games in a season since [Dizzy Dean](/wiki/Dizzy_Dean "Dizzy Dean") in 1934.[[78]](#cite_note-denny-79) St. Louis Cardinals starting pitcher [Bob Gibson](/wiki/Bob_Gibson "Bob Gibson") achieved an equally remarkable feat by allowing an [ERA](/wiki/Earned_run_average "Earned run average") of just 1.12.[[79]](#cite_note-gibson-80)

Following these pitching performances, in December 1968 the MLB Playing Rules Committee voted to reduce the strike zone from knees to shoulders to top of knees to armpits and lower the pitcher's mound from 15 to 10 inches, beginning in the 1969 season.[[80]](#cite_note-mound-81)

In 1973, the American League, which had been suffering from much lower attendance than the National League, sought to increase scoring even further by initiating the [designated hitter](/wiki/Designated_hitter "Designated hitter") (DH) rule.[[81]](#cite_note-dh-82)

### New stadiums and artificial surfaces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: New stadiums and artificial surfaces")]

Throughout the 1960s and 1970s, as baseball expanded, the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) had been surging in popularity, making it economical for many of these cities to build [multi-purpose stadiums](/wiki/Multi-purpose_stadium "Multi-purpose stadium") instead of single-purpose baseball fields. Because of climate and economic issues, many of these facilities had playing surfaces made from [artificial turf](/wiki/Artificial_turf "Artificial turf"), as well as the oval designs characteristic of stadiums designed to house both baseball and football.[[82]](#cite_note-83) This often resulted in baseball fields with relatively more [foul territory](/wiki/Foul_territory "Foul territory") than older stadiums. These characteristics changed the nature of professional baseball, putting a higher premium on speed and defense over home-run hitting power since the fields were often too big for teams to expect to hit many home runs and foul balls hit in the air could more easily be caught for outs.[[83]](#cite_note-84)

Teams began to be built around pitching—particularly their bullpens—and speed on the basepaths. Artificial surfaces meant balls traveled quicker and bounced higher, so it became easier to hit [ground balls](/wiki/Ground_ball "Ground ball") "[in the hole](/wiki/In_the_hole "In the hole")" between the corner and middle infielders. Starting pitchers were no longer expected to throw [complete games](/wiki/Complete_game "Complete game"); it was enough for a starter to pitch 6–7 innings and turn the game over to the team's [closer](/wiki/Closer_(baseball) "Closer (baseball)"), a position which grew in importance over these decades. As stolen bases increased, home run totals dropped. After [Willie Mays](/wiki/Willie_Mays "Willie Mays") hit 52 home runs in 1965, only one player ([George Foster](/wiki/George_Foster_(baseball) "George Foster (baseball)")) reached that mark until the 1990s.[[84]](#cite_note-85)[[85]](#cite_note-86)

### Scandals and a changing game

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Scandals and a changing game")]

During the 1980s, baseball experienced a number of significant changes the game had not seen in years. Home runs were on the decline throughout the decade, with players hitting 40 home runs just 13 times and no one hitting more than 50 home runs in a season for the first time since the dead-ball era (1900–1919).[[86]](#cite_note-parade-87)[[87]](#cite_note-SABR80s-88)

The [1981 MLB strike](/wiki/1981_Major_League_Baseball_strike "1981 Major League Baseball strike") from June 12 until July 31 forced the cancellation of 713 games and resulted in a split-season format.

In 1985, [Pete Rose](/wiki/Pete_Rose "Pete Rose") broke [Ty Cobb](/wiki/Ty_Cobb "Ty Cobb")'s all-time hits record with his 4,192nd hit. In 1989, Rose received a lifetime ban from baseball as a result of betting on baseball games while manager of the [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds"). Rose was the first person to receive a lifetime ban from baseball since 1943.[[86]](#cite_note-parade-87)[[88]](#cite_note-89) 1985 also saw the [Pittsburgh drug trials](/wiki/Pittsburgh_drug_trials "Pittsburgh drug trials") which involved players who were called to testify before a grand jury in Pittsburgh related to cocaine trafficking.

The [1994–95 MLB strike](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_Major_League_Baseball_strike "1994–95 Major League Baseball strike") from August 12, 1994, to April 25, 1995, caused the cancellation of over 900 games and the forfeit of the entire 1994 postseason.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

### Steroid era, further expansion and near contraction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Steroid era, further expansion and near contraction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Mark_mcgwire.jpg/250px-Mark_mcgwire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mark_mcgwire.jpg)

[Mark McGwire](/wiki/Mark_McGwire "Mark McGwire") (batter) was one of several central figures in baseball's steroids scandal.

Routinely in the late 1990s and early 2000s, baseball players hit 40 or 50 home runs in a season, a feat that was considered rare even in the 1980s. It later became apparent that at least some of this power surge was a result of players using [steroids and other performance-enhancing drugs](/wiki/Doping_in_baseball "Doping in baseball").

In 1993, the National League added the [Florida Marlins](/wiki/Florida_Marlins "Florida Marlins") in [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami") and the [Colorado Rockies](/wiki/Colorado_Rockies "Colorado Rockies") in [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver"). In 1998, the Brewers switched leagues by joining the National League, and two new teams were added: the National League's [Arizona Diamondbacks](/wiki/Arizona_Diamondbacks "Arizona Diamondbacks") in [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona") and the American League's [Tampa Bay Devil Rays](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Devil_Rays "Tampa Bay Devil Rays").

After the 2001 season, the team owners [voted in favor of contraction](/wiki/2001_Major_League_Baseball_contraction_plan "2001 Major League Baseball contraction plan"). Several MLB teams had been considered for elimination in early talks about contraction, but the Montreal Expos and the Minnesota Twins were the two teams that came closest to folding under the plan. Plans for MLB contraction were halted when the Twins landlord was awarded a court injunction that required the team to play its 2002 home games at their stadium. MLB owners agreed to hold off on reducing the league's size until at least 2006.[[90]](#cite_note-Plans-91)

The Montreal Expos became the first franchise in over three decades to move when they became the [Washington Nationals](/wiki/Washington_Nationals "Washington Nationals") in 2005. This move left Canada with just one team, but it also returned baseball to Washington after a 33-year absence. This franchise shift, like many previous ones, involved baseball's return to a city that had been previously abandoned.

### The modern game

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The modern game")]

In recent years, with the advent of technologies such as [Statcast](/wiki/Statcast "Statcast") and its use of [Hawk-Eye](/wiki/Hawk-Eye "Hawk-Eye") starting in 2020[[91]](#cite_note-92) as well as with advanced statistics as provided by sites such as [FanGraphs](/wiki/FanGraphs "FanGraphs") and Baseball Savant, MLB has evolved. With the ability to see precise movements of pitchers and batters, teams can assess the mechanics of a player and help them improve. Statcast also provides some features such as tracking the paths of most batted balls, tracking the speed of most batted balls, and tracking the [exit velocity](/wiki/Exit_velocity "Exit velocity") of most batted balls. Statcast has allowed for advanced defensive analytics that have not been possible before tracking of players due to how subjective fielding can be.

The rate of teams using a [defensive shift](/wiki/Defensive_shift "Defensive shift") has gone up from 13.7% in 2016 to 33.6% in 2022 because advanced statistics support this as an effective way to stop hitters from getting hits.[[92]](#cite_note-93) Because the shift reduces the number of balls in play that result in hits and MLB wishes to increase interest in baseball, MLB announced in September 2022 that extreme infield defensive shifts would be banned starting in 2023. To be compliant, there must be two fielders on each side of second base, and those fielders must have both of their feet on the infield dirt at the time the pitch is thrown. If this rule is not followed, the choice of an automatic ball or the outcome of the play is given to the batting team.[[93]](#cite_note-:0-94)

The game of baseball has also slowed down significantly due to an increased number of strikeouts and walks—two outcomes that generally take many pitches to complete—and an increased amount of time taken for a pitcher to pitch. In 2020, it took an average of three hours and six minutes to complete a 9 inning game, a number which has steadily ticked up for years.[[94]](#cite_note-95) Along with the restrictions on defensive shifts, MLB announced the introduction of a [pitch clock](/wiki/Pitch_clock#Major_League_Baseball "Pitch clock") for the 2023 season and beyond, which is something that has been an experiment in MiLB for a few years. The pitch clock starts at 15 seconds. By the time the clock reaches 10 seconds, the catcher must be in their crouch behind home plate. When the clock reaches 8 seconds, the batter must be in the batter's box and be "alert". Before the 15 second timer reaches 0 seconds, the pitcher must have started their "motion to pitch". If any of these deadlines are violated, the count of the batter will be increased by one ball if the defending team violated the pitch clock or one strike if it is the offensive team who violated the pitch clock. Additionally, in order to prevent circumventing these rules, pickoffs and step-offs — which reset the pitch clock — are only allowed two times total per plate appearance, and if a pitcher attempts a third pickoff and fails to get the runner out, a ball will be called. Similarly, a batter is restricted to calling a timeout (an action which resets the pitch clock) just once per plate appearance.[[93]](#cite_note-:0-94)

In 2019, MLB opened an investigation into allegations that members of the [2017 World Series](/wiki/2017_World_Series "2017 World Series") champion [Houston Astros](/wiki/Houston_Astros "Houston Astros") [stole signs](/wiki/Houston_Astros_sign_stealing_scandal "Houston Astros sign stealing scandal") from opposing teams using technology during the 2017 and [2018 seasons](/wiki/2018_Houston_Astros_season "2018 Houston Astros season").[[95]](#cite_note-96) The Astros were found guilty in January 2020 and while no active players faced any repercussions due to an immunity agreement in exchange for testimony, Astros [general manager](/wiki/General_manager_(baseball) "General manager (baseball)") [Jeff Luhnow](/wiki/Jeff_Luhnow "Jeff Luhnow") and [field manager](/wiki/Field_manager "Field manager") [A. J. Hinch](/wiki/A._J._Hinch "A. J. Hinch") were suspended for the entire 2020 season. The Astros were fined the maximum allowable $5 million and forfeited their first- and second-round picks in the 2020 and 2021 drafts.[[96]](#cite_note-wsj12220-97) The [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") were also found guilty of stealing signs during the 2017 and 2018 seasons, with the latter season ending in a World Series victory.[[97]](#cite_note-98) Red Sox manager [Alex Cora](/wiki/Alex_Cora "Alex Cora") was suspended for a year and the team was fined with the loss of a second round draft pick in the 2020 draft as a result.[[98]](#cite_note-99)

### 21st-century moves and potential expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 21st-century moves and potential expansion")]

Main article: [Expansion of Major League Baseball § Potential future expansion](/wiki/Expansion_of_Major_League_Baseball#Potential_future_expansion "Expansion of Major League Baseball")

In November 2023, the [Oakland Athletics relocation to Las Vegas](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics_relocation_to_Las_Vegas "Oakland Athletics relocation to Las Vegas") was approved by MLB owners.[[99]](#cite_note-FeinsandAs-100) They are to play the 2025 through 2027 seasons in [West Sacramento, California](/wiki/West_Sacramento,_California "West Sacramento, California"), while a [new stadium](/wiki/New_Las_Vegas_Stadium "New Las Vegas Stadium") is built in the [Las Vegas metropolitan area](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Valley "Las Vegas Valley"), and will be referred to as simply the "Athletics" and "A's", with no city name attached.

MLB has discussed preparations for another round of expansion, and several investment groups are vying for a franchise. In 2025, commissioner Rob Manfred said MLB could add two teams and pursue a geographic realignment of its leagues and divisions.[[100]](#cite_note-101)

## Organizational structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Organizational structure")]

MLB is governed by the [Major League Baseball Constitution](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_Constitution "Major League Baseball Constitution"). This document has undergone several incarnations since its creation in 1876.[[103]](#cite_note-105) Under the direction of the [commissioner of baseball](/wiki/Commissioner_of_baseball "Commissioner of baseball"), MLB hires and maintains the sport's [umpiring](/wiki/Umpire_(baseball) "Umpire (baseball)") crews, and negotiates marketing, labor, and [television contracts](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_television_contracts "Major League Baseball television contracts"). MLB maintains a unique, controlling relationship over the sport, including most aspects of [Minor League Baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball"). This is due in large part to the 1922 [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court") ruling in *[Federal Baseball Club v. National League](/wiki/Federal_Baseball_Club_v._National_League "Federal Baseball Club v. National League")*, which held that baseball is not [interstate commerce](/wiki/Interstate_commerce "Interstate commerce") and therefore not subject to federal [antitrust](/wiki/Antitrust "Antitrust") law; MLB is the only league that has such a status, and has not faced any competition since this case.[[104]](#cite_note-106)[[105]](#cite_note-107) This ruling has been weakened only slightly in subsequent years.[[106]](#cite_note-Policy_Debate:_Should_the_antitrust_exemption_for_baseball_be_eliminated?-108) The weakened ruling granted more stability to the owners of teams and has resulted in values increasing at double-digit rates.[[106]](#cite_note-Policy_Debate:_Should_the_antitrust_exemption_for_baseball_be_eliminated?-108)[[107]](#cite_note-109) There were several challenges to MLB's primacy in the sport, with notable attempts to establish competing leagues occurring during the late 1800s, from 1913 to 1915 with the short-lived [Federal League](/wiki/Federal_League "Federal League"), and in 1960 with the aborted [Continental League](/wiki/Continental_League "Continental League").[[106]](#cite_note-Policy_Debate:_Should_the_antitrust_exemption_for_baseball_be_eliminated?-108)

The chief executive of MLB is the commissioner, [Rob Manfred](/wiki/Rob_Manfred "Rob Manfred"). The deputy commissioner of baseball administration and chief legal officer is Dan Halem. There are seven other executives: executive vice president and general counsel, chief operations and strategy officer, chief communications officer, chief financial officer and senior advisor, executive vice president and chief marketing officer, chief revenue officer, and chief baseball development officer.[[108]](#cite_note-110)[[109]](#cite_note-execs-111)

The multimedia branch of MLB is [MLB Advanced Media](/wiki/MLB_Advanced_Media "MLB Advanced Media"), which is based in New York City. This branch oversees [MLB.com](/wiki/MLB.com "MLB.com") and each of the 30 teams' websites. Its charter states that MLB Advanced Media holds [editorial independence](/wiki/Editorial_independence "Editorial independence") from the league, but it is under the same ownership group and revenue-sharing plan. MLB Productions is a similarly structured wing of the league, focusing on video and traditional broadcast media. MLB also owns 67 percent of [MLB Network](/wiki/MLB_Network "MLB Network"), with the other 33 percent split between several cable operators and satellite provider [DirecTV](/wiki/DirecTV "DirecTV").[[110]](#cite_note-112) It operates out of studios in [Secaucus, New Jersey](/wiki/Secaucus,_New_Jersey "Secaucus, New Jersey"), and also has editorial independence from the league.[[111]](#cite_note-113)

## League organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: League organization")]

In 1920, the weak National Commission, created to manage relationships between the two leagues, was replaced with the much more powerful commissioner of baseball, who had the power to make decisions for all of professional baseball unilaterally.[[3]](#cite_note-MLBCommissioner-3) From 1901 to 1960, the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") and [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") fielded eight teams apiece.

In the 1960s, MLB expansion added eight teams, including the first non-U.S. team (the [Montreal Expos](/wiki/Montreal_Expos "Montreal Expos")). Two teams (the [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/Seattle_Mariners "Seattle Mariners") and the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays")) were also added in the 1970s. From 1969 through 1993, each league consisted of an East and West Division. In 1993, the National League expanded with two teams, the [Florida Marlins](/wiki/Florida_Marlins "Florida Marlins") and the [Colorado Rockies](/wiki/Colorado_Rockies "Colorado Rockies"), to even up the number of teams in both leagues. A third division, the Central Division, was formed in each league in 1994. Until 1996, the two leagues met on the field only during the [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") and the [All-Star Game](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "Major League Baseball All-Star Game"). Regular-season [interleague play](/wiki/Interleague_play "Interleague play") was introduced in 1997.[[112]](#cite_note-Interleague-114)

In March 1995, two new franchises, the [Arizona Diamondbacks](/wiki/Arizona_Diamondbacks "Arizona Diamondbacks") and Tampa Bay Devil Rays (now known as the [Tampa Bay Rays](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Rays "Tampa Bay Rays")), were awarded by MLB, which began play in 1998. This addition brought the total number of franchises to 30. In early 1997, MLB decided to assign one new team to each league: Tampa Bay joined the AL and Arizona joined the NL. The original plan was to have an odd number of teams in each league (15 per league, with five in each division), but in order for every team to be able to play daily, this would have required interleague play to be scheduled throughout the entire season. However, it was unclear at the time if the interleague play would continue after the 1998 season, as it had to be approved by [the players' union](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_Players_Association "Major League Baseball Players Association"). For this and other reasons, it was decided that both leagues should continue to have an even number of teams, and therefore, one existing club would have to switch leagues. The [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers#1994–98:_Realignment_/_"We're_taking_this_thing_National" "Milwaukee Brewers") agreed in November 1997 to move from the AL to the NL, thereby making the AL a 14-team league and the NL a 16-team league. At the same time, the [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers") agreed to move from the AL East to the AL Central (to replace Milwaukee), with the expansion Tampa Bay Devil Rays joining the AL East.[[113]](#cite_note-115) Later, when the [Houston Astros](/wiki/Houston_Astros "Houston Astros") changed ownership prior to the 2013 season, the team moved from the NL Central to the AL West,[[114]](#cite_note-116)[[115]](#cite_note-ESPN-AstrosSale2011-117) resulting in both leagues having three divisions of five teams each and allowing all teams to have a more balanced schedule.[[115]](#cite_note-ESPN-AstrosSale2011-117) Interleague play is now held throughout the season.[[115]](#cite_note-ESPN-AstrosSale2011-117)

In 2000, the AL and NL were dissolved as legal entities, and MLB became a single, overall league *de jure*, similar to the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL), [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA) and [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL)—albeit with two components called "leagues" instead of "conferences". The use of "major-league baseball" to refer to the overall AL–NL construct prior to 2000 is preferred, since statements such as "In 1942, [Ted Williams](/wiki/Ted_Williams "Ted Williams") led MLB with 36 homers, 137 RBIs, a .356 batting average, and won the first of his two Triple Crowns" are anachronistic.[[116]](#cite_note-118)

Since becoming a single entity, common [rules](/wiki/Baseball_rules "Baseball rules") and [regulations](/wiki/Regulation_of_sport "Regulation of sport") have been used in both MLB leagues, with one former exception: the AL continued operating under the [designated hitter](/wiki/Designated_hitter "Designated hitter") (DH) rule, while the NL did not.[[117]](#cite_note-DHRule-119)[[118]](#cite_note-Anderson-120) In 2020, the NL used the DH rule for the first time.[[119]](#cite_note-121) As part of the settlement of the [2021–22 Major League Baseball lockout](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Major_League_Baseball_lockout "2021–22 Major League Baseball lockout"), this change was made permanent, thus making the rules in the two leagues identical.[[117]](#cite_note-DHRule-119)[[120]](#cite_note-122)

## Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Cleveland_Indians_22nd_Consecutive_Win_%2837081433606%29.jpg/250px-Cleveland_Indians_22nd_Consecutive_Win_%2837081433606%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cleveland_Indians_22nd_Consecutive_Win_(37081433606).jpg)

[Cleveland Guardians](/wiki/Cleveland_Guardians "Cleveland Guardians") throwback uniform

Main articles: [Major League Baseball uniforms](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_uniforms "Major League Baseball uniforms") and [Baseball uniform](/wiki/Baseball_uniform "Baseball uniform")

A [baseball uniform](/wiki/Baseball_uniform "Baseball uniform") is a type of uniform worn by [baseball players](/wiki/Position_player "Position player") and by some non-playing personnel, such as [field managers](/wiki/Manager_(baseball) "Manager (baseball)") and coaches. It is worn to indicate the person's role in the game and—through the use of logos, colors, and [numbers](/wiki/Uniform_number_(Major_League_Baseball) "Uniform number (Major League Baseball)")—to identify the teams and their players, managers, and coaches.[[121]](#cite_note-123)

Traditionally, home uniforms display the team name on the front, while away uniforms display the team's home location. In modern times, however, exceptions to this pattern have become common, with teams using their team name on both uniforms.[[122]](#cite_note-MLB_Uniforms-124) Most teams also have one or more [alternate uniforms](/wiki/Third_jersey "Third jersey"), usually consisting of the primary or secondary team color on the vest instead of the usual white or gray.[[122]](#cite_note-MLB_Uniforms-124) In the past few decades [throwback uniforms](/wiki/Throwback_uniform "Throwback uniform") have become popular.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

The New York Knickerbockers were the first baseball team to use uniforms, taking the field on April 4, 1849, in pants made of blue wool, white [flannel](/wiki/Flannel "Flannel") shirts ([jerseys](/wiki/Jersey_(sport) "Jersey (sport)")) and [straw hats](/wiki/Straw_hat "Straw hat").[[124]](#cite_note-126)[[125]](#cite_note-127)[[126]](#cite_note-128) [Caps](/wiki/Baseball_cap "Baseball cap") and other types of headgear have been a part of baseball uniforms from the beginning.[[127]](#cite_note-129)[[128]](#cite_note-130) Baseball teams often wore full-brimmed straw hats or no cap at all since there was no official rule regarding headgear.[[129]](#cite_note-131) Under the 1882 uniform rules, players on the same team wore uniforms of different colors and patterns that indicated which position they played. This rule was soon abandoned as impractical.[[130]](#cite_note-The_history_of_the_baseball_uniform_at_the_National_Baseball_Hall_of_Fame-132)

In the late 1880s, [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit_Wolverines "Detroit Wolverines") and [Washington](/wiki/Washington_Nationals_(1886%E2%80%9389) "Washington Nationals (1886–89)") of the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") and [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridegrooms "Brooklyn Bridegrooms") of the American Association were the first to wear striped uniforms.[[131]](#cite_note-133) By the end of the 19th century, teams began the practice of having two different uniforms, one for when they played at home in their own [baseball stadium](/wiki/Baseball_stadium "Baseball stadium") and a different one for when they played away ([on the road](/wiki/Road_game "Road game")) at the other team's ballpark.[[122]](#cite_note-MLB_Uniforms-124) It became common to wear white pants with a white color vest at home and gray pants with a gray or solid (dark) colored vest when away.[[122]](#cite_note-MLB_Uniforms-124) By 1900, both home and away uniforms were standard across the major leagues.[[132]](#cite_note-BAuni-134)

In June 2021, MLB announced a long-term deal with cryptocurrency exchange [FTX](/wiki/FTX "FTX"), which includes the FTX logo appearing on umpire uniforms during all games.[[133]](#cite_note-135) FTX is MLB's first-ever umpire uniform patch partner.[[134]](#cite_note-136) On November 11, 2022, FTX filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection. MLB removed the FTX patches from umpires' uniforms before the 2023 season.[[135]](#cite_note-137)

## Season structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Season structure")]

Main articles: [Major League Baseball schedule](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_schedule "Major League Baseball schedule") and [List of Major League Baseball seasons](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_seasons "List of Major League Baseball seasons")

### Spring training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Spring training")]

Main article: [Spring training](/wiki/Spring_training "Spring training")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Los_Angeles_Dodgers_spring_training_at_Holman_Stadium%2C_Dodgertown%2C_1994.jpg/250px-Los_Angeles_Dodgers_spring_training_at_Holman_Stadium%2C_Dodgertown%2C_1994.jpg)](/wiki/File:Los_Angeles_Dodgers_spring_training_at_Holman_Stadium,_Dodgertown,_1994.jpg)

A Grapefruit League game at the former [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers") camp in [Vero Beach](/wiki/Vero_Beach "Vero Beach"), Florida

[Spring training](/wiki/Spring_training "Spring training") is a series of practices and exhibition games preceding the start of the regular season. Teams hold training camps in the states of Arizona and Florida, where the early warm weather allows teams to practice and play without worrying about the late winter cold. Spring training allows new players to compete for roster and position spots and gives existing team players practice time prior to competitive play. The teams that hold spring training in Arizona are grouped into the [Cactus League](/wiki/Cactus_League "Cactus League"),[[136]](#cite_note-138) while teams that hold camp in Florida are known as the [Grapefruit League](/wiki/Grapefruit_League "Grapefruit League").[[137]](#cite_note-139)

Spring training typically lasts almost two months, starting in mid-February and running until just before the season-opening day, traditionally the first week of April. As pitchers benefit from a longer training period, pitchers and catchers begin spring training several days before the rest of the team.[[138]](#cite_note-140)

### Regular season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Regular season")]

Each team plays 162 games per season. A team's schedule is typically organized into three-game [series](/wiki/Series_(baseball) "Series (baseball)"), sets of consecutive games against the same opponent, with occasional two- or four-game series.[[139]](#cite_note-141) Postponed games or continuations of suspended games can result in an ad hoc one-game or five-game series. All games of a series are usually hosted by the same team, and multiple series are typically grouped together. I.e, a team usually hosts several series in a row, called a homestand, and follows that by going on several road series in a row. Teams generally play games five to seven days per week. Most games are scheduled at night, although teams will often play day games on Opening Day, holidays, and for the last game of a series to allow teams extra time to travel to their next opponent. Sunday games are generally played during the afternoon, allowing teams to travel to their next destination prior to a Monday night game.

In the current three-division structure, each team plays 13 games against each of its four divisional opponents. It plays one home series and one away series, amounting to six or seven games, against the 10 other teams in its league. A team also plays 3-game series with all the other teams in the other league, alternating home teams each year. Furthermore, each team has an interleague "natural rival" (in some cases its counterpart in the same metro area) with which it plays three home games and three away games each year.[[140]](#cite_note-mlb.com-142)

With an odd number of teams in each league (15), it is necessary to have two teams participate in interleague play for most days in the season, except when two or more teams have a day off. From 2013 to 2022, each team played 20 interleague games throughout the season, usually with just one interleague game per day, but for one weekend in late May, all teams would participate in an interleague series. Before 2013, interleague play was structured differently: there would be one weekend in mid-May and another period consisting typically of the last two-thirds of June in which all teams played interleague games (save for two NL teams each day), and no interleague games were scheduled outside those dates. (Season-long interleague play was not necessary before 2013 as each league had an even number of teams. In 2013, the Houston Astros moved to the American League, so that each league would have [15 teams](#League_organization).) Prior to the adoption of the universal designated hitter in 2022, whether the DH was in use depended on whether the home team was from the AL, where the DH was used, or the NL, where it was not.

Starting with the 2023 season, the scheduling formula changed, with each team playing at least one series against every other team every year. Each team plays 13 games against teams in the same division, with one 3-game and one 4-game series at each park, six or seven games against teams within the same league but in other divisions, with one 3-game (or 4-game series) at each park, and one 3-game series against teams in the other league, alternating home teams each year, except for each team's designated interleague rival, which will continue to consist of two 2-game (3-game since 2025) series at each of the teams' home parks.

Beginning with the 2022 season, teams compete for the six [playoff](/wiki/Playoff "Playoff") berths in their respective leagues. To secure a berth, a team must either win its [division](/wiki/MLB#League_organization "MLB") or capture a [wild card spot](/wiki/MLB_Wild_Card "MLB Wild Card") by having one of the three best records among the non-winners in its entire league.[[141]](#cite_note-143) With the adoption of a third wild card, the former practice of breaking ties with an additional regular-season game, known as [Game 163](/wiki/Game_163 "Game 163"), was dropped in favor of a tie-breaker formula.[[142]](#cite_note-144)

### All-Star Game

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: All-Star Game")]

Main article: [Major League Baseball All-Star Game](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "Major League Baseball All-Star Game")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/President_attends_the_32nd_All-Star_Baseball_Game%2C_throws_out_first_ball._Speaker_of_the_House_John_W._McCormack..._-_NARA_-_194250.jpg/250px-President_attends_the_32nd_All-Star_Baseball_Game%2C_throws_out_first_ball._Speaker_of_the_House_John_W._McCormack..._-_NARA_-_194250.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_attends_the_32nd_All-Star_Baseball_Game,_throws_out_first_ball._Speaker_of_the_House_John_W._McCormack..._-_NARA_-_194250.jpg)

President [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") throwing out the [first pitch](/wiki/First_pitch "First pitch") at the 1962 All-Star Game at [DC Stadium](/wiki/DC_Stadium "DC Stadium")

In early-to-mid July, just after the midway point of the season, the Major League Baseball All-Star Game is held during a four-day break from the regular-season schedule. The All-Star Game features a team of players from the American League (AL)—led by the manager of the previous AL World Series team—and a team of players from the National League (NL), similarly managed, in an exhibition game. From 1959 to 1962, two games were held each season, one was held in July and one was held in August. The designated-hitter rule was used in the All-Star Game for the first time in 1989. Following games used a DH when the game was played in an AL ballpark. Since 2010, the DH rule has been in effect regardless of venue.[[143]](#cite_note-145)

The first official All-Star Game was held as part of the [1933 World's Fair](/wiki/1933_World%27s_Fair "1933 World's Fair") in Chicago, Illinois, and was the idea of [Arch Ward](/wiki/Arch_Ward "Arch Ward"), then sports editor for *[The Chicago Tribune](/wiki/The_Chicago_Tribune "The Chicago Tribune")*.[[144]](#cite_note-asgmenu-146) Initially intended to be a one-time event, its great success resulted in making the game an annual one. Ward's contribution was recognized by Major League Baseball in 1962 with the creation of the "Arch Ward Trophy", given to the All-Star Game's Most Valuable Player each year.[[145]](#cite_note-147) (In 1970, it was renamed the Commissioner's Trophy, until 1985, when the name change was reversed. In 2002, it was renamed the [Ted Williams Most Valuable Player Award](/wiki/Ted_Williams_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "Ted Williams Most Valuable Player Award").)[[146]](#cite_note-148)

Beginning in 1947, the eight position players in each team's starting lineup have been voted into the game by fans.[[144]](#cite_note-asgmenu-146) The fan voting was discontinued after a 1957 ballot-box-stuffing scandal in Cincinnati: seven of the eight slots originally went to [Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds") players, two of whom were subsequently removed from the lineup to make room for Willie Mays and [Hank Aaron](/wiki/Hank_Aaron "Hank Aaron"). Fan voting was reinstated in 1970 and has continued ever since, including [internet voting](/wiki/All-Star_Final_Vote "All-Star Final Vote") in recent years.[[147]](#cite_note-149)

The [2002 contest in Milwaukee](/wiki/2002_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "2002 Major League Baseball All-Star Game") controversially ended in an 11-inning tie when both managers ran out of pitchers. In response, starting in 2003 the league which wins the All-Star game received home-field advantage in the World Series: the league champion hosted the first two games at its own ballpark as well as the last two (if necessary). The National League did not win an All-Star game and thus gain a home-field advantage until 2010; it was able to overcome this disadvantage and win in three of the seven World Series from 2003 to 2009.[[148]](#cite_note-150)[[149]](#cite_note-151) This was discontinued after the 2016 season.[[150]](#cite_note-WS_homefield-152)

MLB All-Stars from both leagues have worn uniforms from their respective teams at the game with one exception. In the [1933 All-Star Game](/wiki/1933_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "1933 Major League Baseball All-Star Game"), the National League All-Star Team members wore special gray uniforms with "National League" written in navy blue letters across the front of the jersey.[[151]](#cite_note-The_World_Series_and_Highlights_of_Baseball-153)[[152]](#cite_note-Baseball_uniforms_of_the_20th_century-154)
