The regular season ends after the first Sunday in October (or the last Sunday in September), after which twelve teams enter the postseason playoffs. These twelve teams consist of the six division champions and six "wild-card" teams: the team with the best overall [win–loss record](/wiki/Win%E2%80%93loss_record "Win–loss record") in each of the six divisions, and the three teams in each league with the best records other than the division champions. Four rounds of series of games are played to determine the champion:[[153]](#cite_note-155)

1. [Wild Card Series](/wiki/Wild_Card_Series "Wild Card Series"), a best of three games playoff between the lowest seeded division champion and three "wild-card teams", the higher seeds will host all three games
2. [American League Division Series](/wiki/American_League_Division_Series "American League Division Series") and [National League Division Series](/wiki/National_League_Division_Series "National League Division Series"), each a best-of-five-games series.
3. [American League Championship Series](/wiki/American_League_Championship_Series "American League Championship Series") and [National League Championship Series](/wiki/National_League_Championship_Series "National League Championship Series"), each a best-of-seven-games series played between the winning teams from the Division Series. The league champions are referred to as the [pennant](/wiki/Pennant_(sports) "Pennant (sports)") winners.
4. [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series"), a best-of-seven-games series played between the [pennant](/wiki/Pennant_(sports) "Pennant (sports)") winners of each league.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/World_Series_Trophy_%2848262268286%29.jpg/250px-World_Series_Trophy_%2848262268286%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:World_Series_Trophy_(48262268286).jpg)

The [Commissioner's Trophy](/wiki/Commissioner%27s_Trophy_(MLB) "Commissioner's Trophy (MLB)") is awarded annually to the winning team of the [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series"), the league's championship series that concludes the postseason.

Within each league, the division winners are the 1, 2, and 3-seeds, based on win–loss records. The teams with the first, second, and third best records among non-division winners will be seeded 4, 5, and 6 respectively. Throughout the postseason, home-field advantage is awarded to the team with the higher win-loss record, with tiebreakers being applied if necessary. In the [Wild Card Series](/wiki/Wild_Card_Series "Wild Card Series"), the 3-seed will host the 6-seed, and the 4-seed will host the 5-seed, with the winners advancing to the [Division Series](/wiki/Division_Series "Division Series"). Unlike the [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL"), there is no reseeding between each series. In the Division Series, the 1-seed goes up against the 4/5-seed and the 2-seed goes up against the 3/6-seed. The winners of those matches will compete in their respective [League Championship Series](/wiki/League_Championship_Series "League Championship Series") for their [league pennant](/wiki/Pennant_(sports)#Baseball "Pennant (sports)"), and the pennant winners will advance to the World Series.[[154]](#cite_note-156) Since 2017, home-field advantage in the World Series is determined by regular-season records of the two league champions, replacing a system used for the prior 14 seasons where the champion of the league that won the All-Star Game would receive home-field advantage.[[150]](#cite_note-WS_homefield-152)

The team with home-field advantage in the Wild Card Series will host all three games, and subsequent series' will split home fields between the two teams in a 2-2-1 format in the Division Series and a 2-3-2 format in the League Championship Series and World Series, with the advantaged team hosting games 1 and 2, as well as 6 and 7 (if necessary). With this format, the home-field advantage does not usually play a large role in the postseason unless the series goes to its maximum number of games. However, because the first two games of a postseason series are hosted by the same team, a team starting with two wins will likely have momentum heading into the venue switch.[[155]](#cite_note-alds-157)[[156]](#cite_note-[[World_Series]]-158)[[157]](#cite_note-159)

## International play

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: International play")]

See also: [List of Major League Baseball games played outside the United States and Canada](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_games_played_outside_the_United_States_and_Canada "List of Major League Baseball games played outside the United States and Canada"), [MLB Japan All-Star Series](/wiki/MLB_Japan_All-Star_Series "MLB Japan All-Star Series"), [MLB Taiwan All-Star Series](/wiki/MLB_Taiwan_All-Star_Series "MLB Taiwan All-Star Series"), and [2019 MLB London Series](/wiki/2019_MLB_London_Series "2019 MLB London Series")

From 1986 to 2018, an MLB [All-Star](/wiki/All-star_game "All-star game") team was sent to a biennial end-of-the-season tour of Japan, dubbed the [MLB Japan All-Star Series](/wiki/MLB_Japan_All-Star_Series "MLB Japan All-Star Series"), playing exhibition games in a [best-of format](/wiki/Playoff_format#Best-of_formats "Playoff format") against the All-Stars from [Nippon Professional Baseball](/wiki/Nippon_Professional_Baseball "Nippon Professional Baseball") (NPB) or, in 2014 and 2018, their national team, [Samurai Japan](/wiki/Samurai_Japan "Samurai Japan").

In 2008, MLB played the [MLB China Series](/wiki/MLB_China_Series "MLB China Series") in the People's Republic of China. It was a series of two spring-training games between the San Diego Padres and Los Angeles Dodgers. The games were an effort to popularize baseball in China.[[158]](#cite_note-160)

MLB played the [MLB Taiwan All-Star Series](/wiki/MLB_Taiwan_All-Star_Series "MLB Taiwan All-Star Series") in Taiwan in November 2011. It was a series of five exhibition games played by a team made up of MLB players called the MLB All-Stars and the [Chinese Taipei national baseball team](/wiki/Chinese_Taipei_national_baseball_team "Chinese Taipei national baseball team"). The MLB All-Stars swept the series, five games to zero.[[159]](#cite_note-161) At the end of the 2011 season, it was announced that the [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/Seattle_Mariners "Seattle Mariners") and the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics "Oakland Athletics") would play their season openers in Japan.[[160]](#cite_note-162) In October 2013, Phil Rogers of the *[Chicago Tribune](/wiki/Chicago_Tribune "Chicago Tribune")* wrote that MLB was considering postseason all-star tours in Taiwan and Korea; baseball is increasing in popularity in both countries.[[161]](#cite_note-Jarosh-163)

The [Arizona Diamondbacks](/wiki/Arizona_Diamondbacks "Arizona Diamondbacks") opened the 2014 season against [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers") from March 22–23 in Australia.[[162]](#cite_note-164) The teams played each other at the historic [Sydney Cricket Ground](/wiki/Sydney_Cricket_Ground "Sydney Cricket Ground"), which has a seating capacity of 46,000. The two games represented the first MLB regular-season play held in that country. The games counted as home games for the Diamondbacks, so they played 79 home games at [Chase Field](/wiki/Chase_Field "Chase Field").[[163]](#cite_note-165)

In 2019, the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") were the home team in a regular-season two-game series against the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees"). The games, which were the first regular-season MLB games held in Europe, were played on June 29–30 at [London Stadium](/wiki/London_Stadium "London Stadium") with the Yankees winning both games.[[164]](#cite_note-166)

The [San Diego Padres](/wiki/San_Diego_Padres "San Diego Padres") and Los Angeles Dodgers opened the 2024 regular season on March 20–21 at [Gocheok Sky Dome](/wiki/Gocheok_Sky_Dome "Gocheok Sky Dome") in Seoul, South Korea. The ceremonial first pitch of the opening game was thrown by the first Korean-born MLB player [Chan Ho Park](/wiki/Chan_Ho_Park "Chan Ho Park"), who made his debut with the LA Dodgers in 1994. The two-game Seoul Series resulted in a split between the two teams. There were also four exhibition games played during March 17–18, where the Dodgers took on the [Kiwoom Heroes](/wiki/Kiwoom_Heroes "Kiwoom Heroes") and Team Korea, and the Padres faced the [LG Twins](/wiki/LG_Twins "LG Twins") and Team Korea.[[165]](#cite_note-167)

The 2025 season began with the Los Angeles Dodgers and the [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") playing in Tokyo at the [Tokyo Dome](/wiki/Tokyo_Dome "Tokyo Dome") for the [MLB Tokyo Series 2025](/wiki/MLB_Tokyo_Series_2025 "MLB Tokyo Series 2025").[[166]](#cite_note-:1-168)[[93]](#cite_note-:0-94)

Together with the [World Baseball Softball Confederation](/wiki/World_Baseball_Softball_Confederation "World Baseball Softball Confederation"), MLB sponsors the [World Baseball Classic](/wiki/World_Baseball_Classic "World Baseball Classic"), an international baseball tournament contested by national teams.[[167]](#cite_note-169)

## Performance-enhancing drugs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Performance-enhancing drugs")]

See also: [Doping in baseball](/wiki/Doping_in_baseball "Doping in baseball") and [List of Major League Baseball players suspended for performance-enhancing drugs](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_players_suspended_for_performance-enhancing_drugs "List of Major League Baseball players suspended for performance-enhancing drugs")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Palmeiro_swing2.png/250px-Palmeiro_swing2.png)](/wiki/File:Palmeiro_swing2.png)

[Rafael Palmeiro](/wiki/Rafael_Palmeiro "Rafael Palmeiro") (batter) was one of the MLB players suspended for steroid use.[[168]](#cite_note-170)

In 1998, both [Mark McGwire](/wiki/Mark_McGwire "Mark McGwire") and [Sammy Sosa](/wiki/Sammy_Sosa "Sammy Sosa") hit more [home runs](/wiki/Home_run "Home run") than the record of 61 set by Yankees right fielder [Roger Maris](/wiki/Roger_Maris "Roger Maris") in 1961. [Barry Bonds](/wiki/Barry_Bonds "Barry Bonds") topped the record in 2001 with 73 home runs. McGwire, Bonds, and Sosa became the subjects of speculation regarding the use of performance-enhancing substances. McGwire later admitted that he used a steroid hormone that was still legal in baseball during the 1998 season.[[169]](#cite_note-ESPNsteroids-171) Baseball's original steroid testing policy, in effect from 2002 to 2005, provided for penalties ranging from a ten-game suspension for a first positive test to a one-year suspension for a fourth positive test. Players were tested at least once per year, with the chance that several players could be tested many times per year.[[170]](#cite_note-wnbc-172)

A 2006 book, *[Game of Shadows](/wiki/Game_of_Shadows "Game of Shadows")* by *[San Francisco Chronicle](/wiki/San_Francisco_Chronicle "San Francisco Chronicle")* investigative reporters [Lance Williams and Mark Fainaru-Wada](/wiki/Lance_Williams_and_Mark_Fainaru-Wada "Lance Williams and Mark Fainaru-Wada"), chronicled alleged extensive use of performance enhancers, including several types of steroids and [growth hormone](/wiki/Growth_hormone "Growth hormone") by baseball superstars Barry Bonds, [Gary Sheffield](/wiki/Gary_Sheffield "Gary Sheffield"), and [Jason Giambi](/wiki/Jason_Giambi "Jason Giambi"). Former [Senate Majority Leader](/wiki/Senate_Majority_Leader "Senate Majority Leader") George Mitchell was appointed by Selig on March 30, 2006[[171]](#cite_note-MLB_pre-173) to investigate the use of performance-enhancing drugs in MLB. The appointment was made after several influential members of the [U.S. Congress](/wiki/U.S._Congress "U.S. Congress") made negative comments about both the effectiveness and honesty of MLB's [drug policies](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_drug_policy "Major League Baseball drug policy") and Commissioner Selig.[[172]](#cite_note-NYT_20071213-174)

The day before the [Mitchell Report](/wiki/Mitchell_Report "Mitchell Report") was to be released in 2007, Selig said, "I haven't seen the report yet, but I'm proud I did it."[[173]](#cite_note-Chicago_Tribune_20071212-175)[[174]](#cite_note-espn-176) The report said that after mandatory random testing began in 2004, [HGH treatment for athletic enhancement](/wiki/HGH_treatment_for_athletic_enhancement "HGH treatment for athletic enhancement") became popular among players, as HGH is not detectable in tests. It pointed out that HGH is likely a placebo with no performance-enhancing effects.[[175]](#cite_note-report_pg_SR2-177) The report included substance use allegations against at least one player from each MLB team.[[176]](#cite_note-178)

According to [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN"), some people questioned whether Mitchell's director role with the Boston Red Sox created a conflict of interest, especially because no "prime [Sox] players were in the report".[[177]](#cite_note-report_pg_A1-179) The report named several prominent Yankees who were parts of World Series clubs; there is a long-running and fierce [Yankees–Red Sox rivalry](/wiki/Yankees%E2%80%93Red_Sox_rivalry "Yankees–Red Sox rivalry"). Former U.S. prosecutor [John M. Dowd](/wiki/John_M._Dowd "John M. Dowd") brought up Mitchell's conflict of interest,[[178]](#cite_note-180) but he later said that the former senator had done a good job.[[179]](#cite_note-181) Mitchell acknowledged that his "tight relationship with Major League Baseball left him open to criticism",[[180]](#cite_note-gregjohnson-182) but he said that readers who examine the report closely "will not find any evidence of bias, of special treatment of the Red Sox".[[180]](#cite_note-gregjohnson-182)

On January 10, 2013, MLB and the players union reached an agreement to add random, in-season HGH testing. They also agreed to implement a new test to reveal the use of [testosterone](/wiki/Testosterone_(medication) "Testosterone (medication)") for the 2013 season.[[181]](#cite_note-183) The current MLB drug policy provides for an 80-game suspension for a first positive test, a 162-game suspension for a second positive test, and a lifetime suspension for a third positive test.[[182]](#cite_note-184) In 2009, allegations surfaced against [Alex Rodriguez](/wiki/Alex_Rodriguez "Alex Rodriguez") and [David Ortiz](/wiki/David_Ortiz "David Ortiz"), and [Manny Ramirez](/wiki/Manny_Ramirez "Manny Ramirez") received a 50-game suspension after testing positive for banned substances. In early April 2011, Ramirez retired from baseball rather than face a 100-game suspension for his second positive steroid test.[[183]](#cite_note-185) He would later unretire, having the suspension dropped to 50 games, and would serve those in 2012.[[184]](#cite_note-186)

## Media coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Media coverage")]

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Television")]

Main article: [Major League Baseball on television](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_on_television "Major League Baseball on television")

See also: [List of current Major League Baseball broadcasters](/wiki/List_of_current_Major_League_Baseball_broadcasters "List of current Major League Baseball broadcasters")

#### Linear

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Linear")]

Starting in 2026, [Fox](/wiki/MLB_on_Fox "MLB on Fox"), [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN_Major_League_Baseball "ESPN Major League Baseball"), [NBC](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_on_NBC "Major League Baseball on NBC"), [TBS](/wiki/MLB_on_TBS "MLB on TBS"), and [MLB Network](/wiki/MLB_Network "MLB Network") will nationally televise MLB games in the United States. Fox has been MLB's primary broadcast television partner since 1996. Since 2008, Fox has broadcast MLB games on [Saturdays](/wiki/Baseball_Night_in_America "Baseball Night in America") throughout the entire season; Fox previously only broadcast games from May to September.[[185]](#cite_note-fox-187) Fox also holds rights to the All-Star Game each season. Fox also alternates League Championship Series broadcasts, broadcasting the [American League Championship Series](/wiki/American_League_Championship_Series "American League Championship Series") (ALCS) in odd-numbered years and the [National League Championship Series](/wiki/National_League_Championship_Series "National League Championship Series") (NLCS) in even-numbered years. Fox broadcasts all games of the World Series.[[186]](#cite_note-188)[[187]](#cite_note-189)[[188]](#cite_note-190)

ESPN has been MLB's cable television partner since 1990.[[189]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-191) The network has aired the weekly regular season game *[Sunday Night Baseball](/wiki/Sunday_Night_Baseball "Sunday Night Baseball")* and postseason broadcasts of the Wild Card Series exclusively.[[166]](#cite_note-:1-168)[[190]](#cite_note-:2-192)

Prior to the 2026 season, MLB and ESPN exercised a mutual opt-out on their deal that would have ended in 2028; NBC agreed to take over *Sunday Night Baseball* and the Wild Card games in the interim while ESPN signed a restructured deal for a package of midweek games on its linear television services.[[9]](#cite_note-NewMLBTVContract2026-9)[[10]](#cite_note-MLBTV2026-10)[[191]](#cite_note-sbj-2026deals-193)

TBS began airing Sunday afternoon regular season games (non-exclusive) nationally in 2008, which was then replaced by Tuesday night games in 2022.[[192]](#cite_note-:3-194)[[193]](#cite_note-195) From 2007 to 2020, TBS had its exclusive rights to any tiebreaker games that determine division or wild card champions. It also airs exclusive coverage of the [Division Series](/wiki/Division_Series "Division Series") round of the playoffs.[[194]](#cite_note-tbs-196) TBS carries the [League Championship Series](/wiki/League_Championship_Series "League Championship Series") that are not included under Fox's television agreement; TBS shows the NLCS in odd-numbered years and the ALCS in even-numbered years.[[192]](#cite_note-:3-194)

In January 2009, MLB launched MLB Network, featuring news and coverage from around the league, and airing 26 live games in the 2009 season.[[195]](#cite_note-washpost-197) Each team also has local broadcasts for all games not exclusively carried nationally. These games are typically split between a local broadcast television station and a local or [regional sports network](/wiki/Regional_sports_network "Regional sports network") (RSN), though some teams only air local games through RSNs or through their own team networks.

As Canada only contains one team, [Sportsnet](/wiki/Sportsnet "Sportsnet") broadcasts Toronto Blue Jays games nationally.[[196]](#cite_note-Sportsnet_will_carry_all_162_games_for_Blue_Jays-198) The channel is owned by [Rogers Communications](/wiki/Rogers_Communications "Rogers Communications"), who is also the parent company of the Blue Jays.[[196]](#cite_note-Sportsnet_will_carry_all_162_games_for_Blue_Jays-198) Sportsnet also televises Fox's Saturday afternoon games, the All-Star Game, playoff games, and the World Series.[[196]](#cite_note-Sportsnet_will_carry_all_162_games_for_Blue_Jays-198)[[197]](#cite_note-Sportsnet-199) In April 2011, [TSN2](/wiki/TSN2 "TSN2") began carrying ESPN *Sunday Night Baseball* in Canada.[[198]](#cite_note-TSN2-200) [TVA Sports](/wiki/TVA_Sports "TVA Sports") airs Blue Jays games in French.[[199]](#cite_note-201)

#### Streaming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Streaming")]

Several MLB games are broadcast exclusively on [streaming television](/wiki/Streaming_television "Streaming television"). After a year of exclusive games on Facebook,[[200]](#cite_note-202)[[201]](#cite_note-203) MLB partnered with [YouTube](/wiki/MLB_Game_of_the_Week_Live_on_YouTube "MLB Game of the Week Live on YouTube") to stream weekly games on the service beginning in the 2019 season, and extending until 2022.[[202]](#cite_note-204)[[203]](#cite_note-205)[[204]](#cite_note-206) In 2022, MLB made a deal with [Apple Inc.](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") to launch *[Friday Night Baseball](/wiki/Friday_Night_Baseball "Friday Night Baseball")* on its [Apple TV+](/wiki/Apple_TV%2B "Apple TV+") streaming service, and NBC Sports to broadcast *[MLB Sunday Leadoff](/wiki/MLB_Sunday_Leadoff "MLB Sunday Leadoff")*, a package of early Sunday afternoon games on [Peacock](/wiki/Peacock_(streaming_service) "Peacock (streaming service)").[[205]](#cite_note-207)[[206]](#cite_note-208)[[207]](#cite_note-209)[[208]](#cite_note-210)

Since the [2023 season's](/wiki/2023_Major_League_Baseball_season "2023 Major League Baseball season") Division Series, [HBO Max](/wiki/HBO_Max "HBO Max") began simulcasting games broadcast by its sister network TBS (both owned by [Warner Bros. Discovery](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Discovery "Warner Bros. Discovery")).[[209]](#cite_note-211)

In the [2024 season](/wiki/2024_Major_League_Baseball_season "2024 Major League Baseball season"), [The Roku Channel](/wiki/The_Roku_Channel "The Roku Channel") began streaming *MLB Sunday Leadoff*.[[210]](#cite_note-Roku-212) Midway through that season, it was confirmed that [TelevisaUnivision](/wiki/TelevisaUnivision "TelevisaUnivision") had signed a multi-year agreement to broadcast *MLB En Vivo* on Tuesdays on [UniMás](/wiki/UniM%C3%A1s "UniMás"), [TUDN](/wiki/TUDN "TUDN") and [ViX](/wiki/Vix_(streaming_service) "Vix (streaming service)"), with live clips and highlights of up to 15 games each week.The contract also included exclusive Spanish-language TV rights to the 2024 American League postseason games (ALDS and ALCS) with select games airing on [Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision") as well as UniMás, TUDN and Vix, and Game 1 of that season's World Series, sharing airtime with Fox and [Fox Deportes](/wiki/Fox_Deportes "Fox Deportes").[[211]](#cite_note-213)[[212]](#cite_note-214)

In [2026 season](/wiki/2026_Major_League_Baseball_season "2026 Major League Baseball season"), Peacock will regain the *MLB Sunday Leadoff* package, while [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") will stream the [Home Run Derby](/wiki/Home_Run_Derby "Home Run Derby") (taking over from ESPN) and the opening night of the season.[[191]](#cite_note-sbj-2026deals-193)

### Blackout policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Blackout policy")]

Main article: [Major League Baseball blackout policy](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_blackout_policy "Major League Baseball blackout policy")

See also: [Syndication exclusivity](/wiki/Syndication_exclusivity "Syndication exclusivity")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/MLB_Blackout_Areas.svg/500px-MLB_Blackout_Areas.svg.png)](/wiki/File:MLB_Blackout_Areas.svg)

MLB blackout map in the United States

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Canadian_MLB_blackout_area.svg/500px-Canadian_MLB_blackout_area.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Canadian_MLB_blackout_area.svg)

Canadian regions subject to

Toronto Blue Jays exclusively

Shared with [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/Seattle_Mariners "Seattle Mariners")

Shared with [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/Minnesota_Twins "Minnesota Twins")

Shared with [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox")

Note: Toronto Blue Jays territory covers all of Canada

MLB has several [blackout](/wiki/Blackout_(broadcasting) "Blackout (broadcasting)") rules.[[213]](#cite_note-215) A local broadcaster has priority to televise games of the team in their market over national broadcasters if the game is not exclusive to the national broadcaster. A market that has a local team playing in a non-exclusive game will receive an alternative programming feed on the national broadcaster.[[214]](#cite_note-Helyar-216) MLB's streaming internet video service is also subject to the same blackout rules.[[215]](#cite_note-217) Commissioner Robert Manfred has expressed interest in changing the blackout policy to loosen the rules for streaming options.[[216]](#cite_note-218)

### Radio and Internet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Radio and Internet")]

Main article: [Major League Baseball on the radio](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_on_the_radio "Major League Baseball on the radio")

The first baseball game ever broadcast on radio was a [Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/1921_Pittsburgh_Pirates_season "1921 Pittsburgh Pirates season") versus [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/1921_Philadelphia_Phillies_season "1921 Philadelphia Phillies season") game on August 5, 1921. The game was broadcast by [KDKA](/wiki/KDKA_(AM) "KDKA (AM)") of [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), and the Pirates defeated the Phillies 8–5. It was broadcast by KDKA staff announcer [Harold Arlin](/wiki/Harold_Arlin "Harold Arlin").[[217]](#cite_note-HB-219)[[218]](#cite_note-TSB-220)[[219]](#cite_note-ASO-221)[[220]](#cite_note-DDO-222)[[221]](#cite_note-223)[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Major_League_Baseball#endnote_WWJ) That year, KDKA and [WJZ](/wiki/WABC_(AM) "WABC (AM)") of [Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey") broadcast the first [World Series](/wiki/1921_World_Series "1921 World Series") on the radio, between the [New York Giants](/wiki/History_of_the_New_York_Giants_(baseball) "History of the New York Giants (baseball)") and the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees"), with [Grantland Rice](/wiki/Grantland_Rice "Grantland Rice") and Tommy Cowan calling the games for KDKA and WJZ, respectively.[[217]](#cite_note-HB-219)[[218]](#cite_note-TSB-220)[[219]](#cite_note-ASO-221) However, the broadcasters were not actually present at the game, but simply gave reports from a [telegraph](/wiki/Telegraph "Telegraph") wire.[[217]](#cite_note-HB-219) In 1922, WJZ broadcast [the entire series](/wiki/1922_World_Series "1922 World Series"), with Rice doing play-by-play.[[218]](#cite_note-TSB-220)[[219]](#cite_note-ASO-221) For the [1923 World Series](/wiki/1923_World_Series "1923 World Series"), Rice was joined on [Westinghouse](/wiki/Westinghouse_Broadcasting "Westinghouse Broadcasting") for the first time by [Graham McNamee](/wiki/Graham_McNamee "Graham McNamee").[[217]](#cite_note-HB-219)[[222]](#cite_note-RW-224)

During the 1923 World Series, Rice was the main broadcaster, but during the fourth inning of Game 3, he turned the microphone over to McNamee.[[219]](#cite_note-ASO-221)[[222]](#cite_note-RW-224) This was the start of McNamee's career, and McNamee became the first [color commentator](/wiki/Color_commentator "Color commentator"). Although frequently criticized for his lack of expertise, McNamee helped popularize baseball.[[223]](#cite_note-225)[[217]](#cite_note-HB-219)[[222]](#cite_note-RW-224)[[224]](#cite_note-Voices-226)

In [1998](/wiki/1998_in_baseball "1998 in baseball"), national radio broadcasts moved to [ESPN Radio](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_on_ESPN_Radio "Major League Baseball on ESPN Radio").[[225]](#cite_note-DBS-227) ESPN Radio currently broadcasts *[Sunday Night Baseball](/wiki/Sunday_Night_Baseball "Sunday Night Baseball")* games during the regular season, as well as Saturday and occasional weekday games, along with the All-Star Game and all postseason contests.[[226]](#cite_note-ESPN-228) Since 2021, [TUDN Radio](/wiki/TUDN_Radio "TUDN Radio") airs Spanish-language coverage of select regular season and postseason games, including the World Series.[[227]](#cite_note-229)

In addition, each team employs its own announcers, who broadcast during the regular season. Most teams operate regional networks to cover their fan bases; some of these supposedly regional networks (such as the [New York Yankees Radio Network](/wiki/New_York_Yankees_Radio_Network "New York Yankees Radio Network")) have a national reach with affiliates located across the United States.[[228]](#cite_note-230) Major League Baseball has an exclusive rights deal with [XM Satellite Radio](/wiki/XM_Satellite_Radio "XM Satellite Radio"), which includes the channel [MLB Network Radio](/wiki/MLB_Network_Radio "MLB Network Radio") and live play-by-play of all games.[[229]](#cite_note-231)

MLB games are also broadcast live on the internet.[[230]](#cite_note-232)[[231]](#cite_note-233) All television and radio broadcasts of games are available via subscription to [MLB.tv](/wiki/MLB.tv "MLB.tv") at Major League Baseball's website, MLB.com, and radio-only broadcasts are also available via subscription to MLB.com Gameday Audio.[[232]](#cite_note-234) In 2026, MLB.tv will be integrated into the [ESPN App](/wiki/ESPN_(streaming_service) "ESPN (streaming service)").[[191]](#cite_note-sbj-2026deals-193)

Since 2005, Major League Baseball has a partnership with [XM Satellite Radio](/wiki/XM_Satellite_Radio "XM Satellite Radio"), launching a 24-7 channel, MLB Home Plate (now [MLB Network Radio](/wiki/MLB_Network_Radio "MLB Network Radio")) which carries every major league game.[[233]](#cite_note-XM-235)[[234]](#cite_note-FAQ-236) Games are also carried on MLB Gameday Audio.[[235]](#cite_note-237)

While all teams maintain a network of stations carrying their games in English, many teams also broadcast their games in Spanish, on a second network. In addition, when the [Washington Nationals](/wiki/Washington_Nationals "Washington Nationals") were based in Montreal as the Montreal Expos, their games were broadcast in both English and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"). Some [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays") games have been broadcast in French as well.[[236]](#cite_note-238) Selected games of the Los Angeles Dodgers are broadcast in Korean by [KMPC](/wiki/KMPC "KMPC").[[237]](#cite_note-239)

### Financial landscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Financial landscape")]

Main articles: [Major League Baseball on regional sports networks](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_on_regional_sports_networks "Major League Baseball on regional sports networks") and [Cord-cutting](/wiki/Cord-cutting "Cord-cutting")

In modern years, teams have become increasingly reliant on local cable television rights fees, which grew unstable in the 2020s and which are not subject to equal revenue sharing. Following the [Disney-Fox merger](/wiki/Disney-Fox_merger "Disney-Fox merger"), Disney divested Fox's regional broadcasting networks, which covered 14 of MLB's 30 teams, to [Diamond Sports Network](/wiki/Main_Street_Sports_Group "Main Street Sports Group") / [Bally Sports](/wiki/Bally_Sports "Bally Sports").[[238]](#cite_note-240)[[239]](#cite_note-:62-241) Diamond filed for bankruptcy in 2023 and took advantage of debtor-friendly rules in the [Bankruptcy Code](/wiki/United_States_Bankruptcy_Code "United States Bankruptcy Code") to cancel or renegotiate its TV deals with a third of the league.[[240]](#cite_note-242) Teams that locked themselves into relatively stable deals near the peak of the market found themselves in a stronger financial position relative to the average team than ever before.[[239]](#cite_note-:62-241)[[241]](#cite_note-243)[[242]](#cite_note-:82-244) Critics respond that industry revenues and team valuations are still rising.[[242]](#cite_note-:82-244)[[243]](#cite_note-245)[[244]](#cite_note-246)

### International broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: International broadcasting")]

[Disney Media Networks](/wiki/Disney_Media_Networks "Disney Media Networks") Latin America through its linear channels ([ESPN](/wiki/ESPN_(Latin_America) "ESPN (Latin America)"), [ESPN Caribbean](/wiki/ESPN_Caribbean "ESPN Caribbean"), [ESPN Brazil](/wiki/ESPN_Brazil "ESPN Brazil")) and [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+") owns the broadcast rights to more than 180 regular season games (at least 1 game per day), the Home Run Derby, the All Star Game and the post season including: the Wild Card Series, the Division Series, the Championship Series and the World Series for all of Latin America and the Caribbean.[[245]](#cite_note-247)[[246]](#cite_note-248)[[247]](#cite_note-249)[[248]](#cite_note-250) ESPN broadcasts some MLB games in other countries, including [ESPN Australia](/wiki/ESPN_Australia "ESPN Australia") in Australia and New Zealand, [ESPN Africa](/wiki/ESPN_Africa "ESPN Africa"), and [ESPN in the Netherlands](/wiki/ESPN_(Netherlands) "ESPN (Netherlands)").[[249]](#cite_note-251)[[250]](#cite_note-:5-252)

In Mexico, until 2022, [Fox Sports](/wiki/Fox_Sports "Fox Sports") aired five regular season games per week[[251]](#cite_note-253) and post-season games that belong to the league that broadcasts its American namesake (including the [Wild Card Series](/wiki/Wild_Card_Series "Wild Card Series")). Likewise, [TUDN](/wiki/TUDN "TUDN") carried 4 matches a week until 2022: through the pay television signal, games were broadcast on Tuesdays and Thursdays, while on Saturdays (generally at 12:00 p.m.) and Sundays (rotating between NBA games, until the start of the NFL season) games were broadcast on [Canal 9](/wiki/Canal_9_(Danish_TV_channel) "Canal 9 (Danish TV channel)"). Starting with the Championship Series, it only broadcast one division and the whole World Series, these were broadcast on free-to-air television for 59 years.[[252]](#cite_note-254) Starting with the 2023 season, [Imagen Televisión](/wiki/Imagen_Televisi%C3%B3n "Imagen Televisión") announced it would begin broadcasting a regular season game every Saturday and Sunday as well as the playoffs and the World Series on open television and its website in [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico").[[253]](#cite_note-255)[[254]](#cite_note-256)

In Argentina, [Fox Sports](/wiki/Fox_Sports_(Argentina) "Fox Sports (Argentina)") carries 5 regular season games per week and post-season games that belong to the league that broadcasts its American namesake (including the Wild Card Series).[[255]](#cite_note-257)

In Venezuela, since the 2016 season the cable channel IVC has the broadcast rights, they currently air 7 games per week.[[256]](#cite_note-258) Starting with the 2022 season, [Televen](/wiki/Televen "Televen") broadcasts two games a week and the postseason on free-to-air TV[[257]](#cite_note-259)[[258]](#cite_note-260)[[259]](#cite_note-261) While [Venevisión](/wiki/Venevisi%C3%B3n "Venevisión"), starting with the 2023 season, would return with the space "El Juego de la Semana" that carries a game every Sunday and non-working days on free-to-air television.[[260]](#cite_note-262)

In the Dominican Republic since 2009 [Grupo Altice](/wiki/Altice_Dominicana_S.A. "Altice Dominicana S.A.") has broadcast the entire regular season, playoffs and World Series through pay TV while on free-to-air television and radio [Grupo Corripio](/wiki/Grupo_Corripio "Grupo Corripio") airs 7 games per week and the entire postseason.[[261]](#cite_note-263)[[262]](#cite_note-264)[[263]](#cite_note-265)

In Spain, [Movistar Plus+](/wiki/Movistar_Plus%2B "Movistar Plus+") has the rights to broadcast the entire season, including one game daily, and the postseason, covering all games in the championship series and World Series.[[264]](#cite_note-266)[[250]](#cite_note-:5-252)

[Channel 5](/wiki/Channel_5_(UK) "Channel 5 (UK)") in the United Kingdom previously screened MLB games, including the All-Star Game and [postseason](/wiki/Mlb#Postseason "Mlb") games, on Sunday and Wednesday, usually starting at 1 a.m. [BST](/wiki/British_Summer_Time "British Summer Time"). Most recently, [Johnny Gould](/wiki/Johnny_Gould "Johnny Gould") and [Josh Chetwynd](/wiki/Josh_Chetwynd "Josh Chetwynd") presented *[MLB on Five](/wiki/MLB_on_Five "MLB on Five")* on that station.[[265]](#cite_note-five-267) The channel covered baseball beginning on its opening night in 1997, but for financial reasons, the decision was made not to pick up MLB for the 2009 season.[[266]](#cite_note-268) [BT Sport ESPN](/wiki/BT_Sport_ESPN "BT Sport ESPN") show live and recorded games several times a week—it is available with [BT Sport](/wiki/BT_Sport "BT Sport") and (on a subscriber-basis) [Virgin Media](/wiki/Virgin_Media#Virgin_TV "Virgin Media") in the UK.[[267]](#cite_note-269) [ESPN America](/wiki/ESPN_America "ESPN America") televised many games in the UK and dozens of other countries; in May 2013, ESPN announced that it would shut down the channel on July 31, 2013.[[268]](#cite_note-Spy-270)[[269]](#cite_note-Suspend-271)

In the Middle East, North Africa and France, MLB games are broadcast on [beIN Sports](/wiki/BeIN_Sports_Arabia "BeIN Sports Arabia") channels.[[270]](#cite_note-272)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

In Germany, Austria and Switzerland, as many as 500 MLB games will be broadcast from 2022 exclusively on the [Sport1](/wiki/Sport1_(Germany) "Sport1 (Germany)") platforms until 2026.[[271]](#cite_note-273) [Sport1](/wiki/Sport1_(Eastern_Europe) "Sport1 (Eastern Europe)") also broadcasts MLB games in Hungary, as of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit).[[272]](#cite_note-274)

## Further reading

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Baseball&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Further reading")]

* Banner, Stuart. *The Baseball Trust: A History of Baseball's Antitrust Exemption.* New York: Oxford University Press, 2013.
* [Bouton, Jim](/wiki/Jim_Bouton "Jim Bouton"). *[Ball Four: My Life and Hard Times Throwing the Knuckleball in the Major Leagues](//archive.org/details/ballfourmylifeha00bout "iarchive:ballfourmylifeha00bout")*. World Publishing Company, 1970.
* Buchanan, Lamont, *The World Series and Highlights of Baseball*, E. P. Dutton & Company, 1951.
* Cohen, Richard M., Neft, David, Johnson, Roland T., Deutsch, Jordan A., *The World Series*, 1976, Dial Press.
* Deutsch, Jordan A., [Cohen, Richard M.](/wiki/Richard_M._Cohen "Richard M. Cohen"), [Neft, David](/wiki/Neft,_David "Neft, David"), Johnson, Roland T., *The Scrapbook History of Baseball*, Bobbs-Merrill Company, 1975.
* Scott, Richard. [Jackie Robinson](//archive.org/details/jackierobinson0000scot "iarchive:jackierobinson0000scot"). New York: Chelsea House Publishers, 1987.
* [James, Bill](/wiki/James,_Bill "James, Bill"). *The Historical Baseball Abstract*. New York: Villard, 1985 (with many subsequent editions).
* [Lanigan, Ernest](/wiki/Ernest_Lanigan "Ernest Lanigan"), *Baseball Cyclopedia*, 1922, originally published by *Baseball Magazine*.
* Lansch, Jerry, *Glory Fades Away: The Nineteenth Century World Series Rediscovered*, Taylor Publishing, 1991. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-87833-726-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-87833-726-1 "Special:BookSources/0-87833-726-1").
* Murphy, Cait. *Crazy '08: How a Cast of Cranks, Rogues, Boneheads, and Magnates Created the Greatest Year in Baseball History.* New York: Smithsonian Books, 2007. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-06-088937-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-06-088937-1 "Special:BookSources/978-0-06-088937-1").
* Okkonen, Marc. *Baseball Uniforms of the 20th Century: The Official Major League Baseball Guide*, 1991.
* [Ritter, Lawrence](/wiki/Lawrence_Ritter "Lawrence Ritter"). *[The Glory of their Times](/wiki/The_Glory_of_Their_Times "The Glory of Their Times")*. New York: MacMillan, 1966. Revised edition, New York: William Morrow, 1984.
* Ross, Brian. "Band of Brothers". *Minor League News*, April 6, 2005. [Archived version](https://web.archive.org/web/20090221185144/http://minorleaguenews.com/history/baseball/2005/04/06/01.html).
* Seymour, Harold. *Baseball: The Early Years*. 2v. New York: Oxford University Press, 1960. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-19-500100-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-19-500100-1 "Special:BookSources/0-19-500100-1").
* [Turkin, Hy](/wiki/Hy_Turkin "Hy Turkin"), and [Thompson, S. C.](/wiki/S._C._Thompson "S. C. Thompson"), *The Official Encyclopedia of Baseball*, 1951, A.S. Barnes and Company
* Tygiel, Jules. *Past Time: Baseball as History*. New York: Oxford University Press, 2000. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-19-514604-2](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-19-514604-2 "Special:BookSources/0-19-514604-2").
* *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, *The Complete Book of Baseball: A Scrapbook History*, 1980, Bobbs Merrill.
